one_line_core: 开源 LLM 代理编程能力正从静态基准转向实战化验证，但自我改进机制与可靠性之间的鸿沟仍是最大挑战。
executive_summary: Hugging Face 提出在用户自定义工具链上评估开源模型的 Agentic 能力，揭示当前模型在复杂自主规划上的短板。
Ornith-1.0 试图通过自我脚手架和自我改进机制突破代理编码上限，目前仍处于概念验证阶段。
三篇主题共同指向一个核心矛盾：开源模型在代理场景中的理论扩展性 vs 生产环境下对稳定性和确定性的刚性需求。

key_points.title: Agent 能力评估范式转变：从标准化基准到自有工具链实战
key_points.why_it_matters: 评估越贴近真实业务环境，越能暴露模型在自主决策、错误恢复等深层代理能力上的不足，驱动从“刷分”到“真用”的跨越。

key_points.title: Ornith-1.0 探索自我脚手架与自我改进的无人干预编码代理
key_points.why_it_matters: 若能在开源社区形成反馈闭环并持续提高任务成功率，有望为低成本、透明可控的代理编程打开新路径，但目前可靠性仍是巨大瓶颈。

key_points.title: 开源与闭源模型在代理编程战场的非对称竞争
key_points.why_it_matters: 开源模型缺少闭源模型的专有数据飞轮和集中式安全对齐，但在生态多样性和透明性上构建护城河，谁能定义‘足够好的代理’，谁将定义下一阶段工具链标准。

key_insights: Agent 能力的真正分水岭不是工具调用，而是自主决定何时、如何使用工具以及从错误中恢复的元认知能力，当前开源模型在这方面仍显著落后。
Ornith-1.0 的核心价值不在于静态编码基准得分，而在于它能否跑通‘开源社区贡献反馈—模型自我进化—任务成功率提升’的低成本强化学习闭环。
谁能在自己的工具链上定义‘agentic enough’，谁就掌握了 AI 代理在垂直行业落地的标准制定权，评估本身正成为新的战略高地。
limitations: 所有分析均基于公开博客和 GitHub 发布信息，缺少对模型能力的独立实测验证。
Ornith-1.0 尚处于早期版本，其自我改进机制的实际效果和在复杂真实任务中的表现缺乏长期跟踪数据。
社区反馈（Hacker News 分数和评论）只能反映开发者初步兴趣，不代表技术成熟度或实际应用价值。
actions: 关注 Hugging Face 等社区对 Agentic 能力评估框架的后续细化和标准化动向。
追踪 Ornith-1.0 开源社区贡献和模型更新频率，测试其在特定内部工具链上的任务完成率。
评估组织内部是否具备将自有工具、API 和工作流纳入代理能力测试的技术条件，提前布局实战化 Agent 评估能力。

facts: Ornith-1.0 是一个名为 'Self-scaffolding LLMs for agentic coding' 的项目，发布于 deep-reinforce.com
该项目在 Hacker News 获得 52 分和 7 条评论，显示一定的社区关注度
核心概念是基于大型语言模型的自我脚手架能力，用于实现自主编码代理
contradictions: 自主构建的灵活性与输出可控性之间的矛盾
模型自我改进的潜力与当前能力瓶颈之间的矛盾
新型代理框架的吸引力与成熟开发工具（如 Devin, Copilot）的竞争压力之间的矛盾
自脚手架方法的泛化能力与具体工程任务复杂性之间的矛盾
analyses.primary_contradiction: LLM 自脚手架的理论扩展性 vs 实际编码环境下的鲁棒性与可靠性要求
analyses.core_insight: Ornith-1.0 试图通过让 LLM 自主构建提示或工具链来突破代理编码的上限，目前仍处于早期概念验证，需解决自举可靠性和工程落地问题。
analyses.title: Ornith-1.0: Self-scaffolding LLMs for agentic coding

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
support_evidence.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

support_evidence.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
support_evidence.summary:
Artificial intelligence is driving a revolution in scientific discovery, accelerating everything from hypothesis generation to mathematical theorem proving. However, this rapid acceleration is creating a systemic challenge: traditional human peer review cannot scale to match the influx of AI-assisted science. Ultimately, to resolve this tension, we must also deploy AI to accelerate the verification and review process itself. To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

facts: Hugging Face 发布了题为《Is it agentic enough? Benchmarking open models on your own tooling》的博客文章，聚焦于评估 AI 模型的 Agent 能力。
该工作强调在用户自定义工具（your own tooling）上对开源模型进行基准测试，而非依赖固定的标准化评估集。
评估的核心问题是探查开源模型是否具备足够的‘代理性’（agentic enough），即自主规划、使用工具和完成复杂任务的能力。
文章来源于 Hugging Face 官方博客，属于一线 AI 开源社区的工程实践与评估研究。
contradictions: Agent 能力期望 vs 实际表现：业界对 AI 模型自主执行任务的期望很高，但实际开源模型在复杂工具调用和多步推理上仍存在显著短板。
定制化评估 vs 标准化基准：在‘你自己的工具’上进行测试更贴近真实应用，但这与追求可复现、可比较的标准化基准测试之间存在权衡。
开源模型 vs 闭源模型：开源模型在生态和透明度上占优，但在工具使用的稳定性和高级 Agent 规划能力上通常被认为落后于最先进的闭源模型。
工具调用能力 vs 自主决策能力：使用工具完成任务是 Agent 的表层能力，而自主决定何时使用何种工具、纠错和规划属于深层决策能力，两者的发展步调可能不一致。
analyses.primary_contradiction: 真实的 Agent 任务需求与当前开源模型底层推理/规划能力不足之间的矛盾。标题中的‘is it agentic enough’表明，核心冲突不是能否调用工具，而是能否像真正的 Agent 一样稳定、自主地解决复杂现实问题。
analyses.core_insight: Agent 评估正从基准刷分转向基于具体业务工具的实战化考验，这标志着 AI 生态开始正视‘代理能力’中真实性与可靠性之间的鸿沟，谁能在自己的工具链上定义‘足够好’，谁就掌握了 Agent 落地的标准制定权。

facts: DeepReinforce AI 在 GitHub 上发布了 Ornith-1.0，定位为用于代理式编程（agentic coding）的开源模型。
该模型明确宣称具备“自我提升”（self-improving）能力。
发布信息在 Hacker News 上获得了 141 分和 30 条评论，表明在开发者群体中引起了一定的初步关注。
项目处于 1.0 版本，暗示这是一个早期里程碑，可能尚未经历大规模生产环境验证。
contradictions: 开源透明性与对抗 Devin 等拥有大量专有数据和资金壁垒的闭源代理编程模型时的竞争力不足。
模型自我提升的迭代自由度与代理编程任务对确定性、稳定输出的高要求之间的冲突。
代理式编程需要长链推理与环境交互能力，与当前开源模型在处理复杂长程任务时容易产生幻觉和逻辑断裂的天然限制。
通过发布 MVP 快速抢占开源生态话语权，与项目早期在社区治理、文档和支持匮乏之间需要长期打磨精力的矛盾。
开源模型难以形成闭源的强力数据飞轮，导致自我提升训练缺乏高质量的私有反馈数据源。
analyses.primary_contradiction: 在没有集中式强力控制和封闭专有数据飞轮的情况下，开源自我提升模型能否在“代理式编程”这一对可靠性和上下文理解要求极高的垂直战场上，建立起足以对抗闭源巨头的非对称优势。
analyses.core_insight: Ornith-1.0 的价值试验不在于静态编码基准得分，而在于它能否跑通“开源社区贡献反馈—模型自我进化—任务成功率提升”的低成本强化学习闭环。
analyses.title: Ornith-1.0: self-improving open-source models for agentic coding

support_evidence.title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
support_evidence.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

support_evidence.title: SRMA-Mamba: Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention Network for Pathological Liver Segmentation in MRI Volumes
support_evidence.summary: Liver cirrhosis plays a critical role in the prognosis of chronic liver disease. Early detection and timely intervention are essential for reducing mortality rates. However, the intricate anatomical architecture and diverse pathological changes of liver tissue complicate the accurate detection and characterization of pathological liver structures in clinical settings. Existing methods underutilize spatial anatomical details in volumetric MRI data, thereby hindering their clinical effectiveness and explainability. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel Mamba-based network, SRMA-Mamba, designed to model the spatial relationships within complex anatomical structures of MRI volumes. By integrating the Spatial Anatomy-Based Mamba module (SABMamba), SRMA-Mamba performs selective Mamba scans within pathological liver tissues and combines anatomical information from the sagittal, coronal, and axial planes to construct a global spatial context representation, enabling efficient volumetric segmentation of pathological liver structures. Furthermore, we introduce the Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention module (SRMA), designed to progressively refine boundary details in the segmentation map, utilizing both the coarse segmentation map and hierarchical encoding features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SRMA-Mamba surpasses state-of-the-art methods, delivering exceptional performance in 3D pathological liver segmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/JunZengz/SRMA-Mamba.

direct_support.title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
direct_support.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

direct_support.summary: Liver cirrhosis plays a critical role in the prognosis of chronic liver disease. Early detection and timely intervention are essential for reducing mortality rates. However, the intricate anatomical architecture and diverse pathological changes of liver tissue complicate the accurate detection and characterization of pathological liver structures in clinical settings. Existing methods underutilize spatial anatomical details in volumetric MRI data, thereby hindering their clinical effectiveness and explainability. By integrating the Spatial Anatomy-Based Mamba module (SABMamba), SRMA-Mamba performs selective Mamba scans within pathological liver tissues and combines anatomical information from the sagittal, coronal, and axial planes to construct a global spatial context representation, enabling efficient volumetric segmentation of pathological liver structures. Furthermore, we introduce the Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention module (SRMA), designed to progressively refine boundary details in the segmentation map, utilizing both the coarse segmentation map and hierarchical encoding features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SRMA-Mamba surpasses state-of-the-art methods, delivering exceptional performance in 3D pathological liver segmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/JunZengz/SRMA-Mamba.

forecast.best_case: Ornith-1.0 迅速积累开源社区的积极贡献，自我脚手架机制在特定编码任务上展示出超过 30% 的自主完成率提升，引发二次传播并吸引更多资源投入，同时 Hugging Face 的自有工具链评估方法被多个大中型企业采用为内部 Agent 能力测试标准，开源模型在实战化评估中缩小与闭源模型的差距。
forecast.worst_case: Ornith-1.0 的自我改进机制在复杂真实场景中产生不可控的退化或幻觉，导致任务完成率极低，社区反馈冷淡，项目停滞；Hugging Face 的评估框架因缺乏统一基准和可比性，难以推广，代理编程的实战化验证仍停留在个别团队的实验阶段，未能形成标准。
forecast.most_likely_case: Ornith-1.0 作为一个早期概念验证，将在开发者圈层中保持一定关注度，但自我改进带来的实际增益有限，无法在短期内达到生产级的可靠性；与此同时，Hugging Face 倡导的‘在自有工具上测评’的思路将激励更多组织构建内部 Agent 评估流水线，但标准化进展缓慢，开源模型代理能力将在未来 6 个月内逐步提升，但仍与闭源模型存在可见差距，生态更偏向‘百花齐放’而非统一突破。
key_variables: Ornith-1.0 自我改进闭环的实际稳定性和任务成功率
社区贡献的活跃度和质量
Hugging Face 评估框架的采纳与标准化进度
开源模型在长链推理和错误恢复能力上的基础研究进展
闭源代理编程工具（如 Devin）的迭代速度与定价策略
forecast.forecast_conclusion: 短期内，开源 LLM 代理编程将在实战化评估的推动下获得更明确的改进方向，但自我改进机制的可靠性瓶颈将使生产落地延迟，生态呈现‘概念热、落地冷’的格局，标准制定权之争进入早期布局阶段。

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
support_evidence.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

support_evidence.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
support_evidence.summary:
Artificial intelligence is driving a revolution in scientific discovery, accelerating everything from hypothesis generation to mathematical theorem proving. However, this rapid acceleration is creating a systemic challenge: traditional human peer review cannot scale to match the influx of AI-assisted science. Ultimately, to resolve this tension, we must also deploy AI to accelerate the verification and review process itself. To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

signals.title: When are LLMs Sufficient Policy Optimizers for Sequential RL Tasks?
signals.summary: We study when large language models (LLMs) can serve as effective black-box policy optimizers for reinforcement learning (RL) tasks, i.e., when can we replace classical RL algorithms with an LLM? We explore this question by introducing Prompted Policy Optimization (PromptPO), an iterative method that prompts an LLM with Python descriptions of the state space, action space, and reward function, then has it generate and refine executable policies based on rollout feedback. Across hard exploration environments, Meta-World robotics tasks, and several real-world control problems, PromptPO often matches or exceeds the performance of standard RL baselines while using substantially fewer environment interactions. To maximize expected return, and without further explicit prompting, the policies PromptPO outputs range from tuned proportional controllers or rule-based plans to policies that run planning algorithms like value iteration. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based policy optimization is sufficient when the LLM can leverage prior knowledge about the environment or optimization strategy. PromptPO underperforms standard RL baselines in MuJoCo domains. This demonstrates possible limitations of LLM-based policy optimization to settings that requiring fine-grained continuous control.

support_evidence.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
support_evidence.summary: Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
support_evidence.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

support_evidence.title: Which Nash Equilibrium? Solver-Dependent Selection on Zero-Sum Nash Polytopes
support_evidence.summary: Many two-player zero-sum games admit not a unique Nash equilibrium but a convex set of them: a polytope of profiles that all share the minimax value V* yet prescribe different behaviour. Standard solvers each converge to some equilibrium and are treated as interchangeable. We ask whether they instead select different members of the Nash set, systematically as a function of the algorithm rather than the seed. Using a tabular, exactly solvable testbed of six games with analytically known Nash sets -- including a two-dimensional Nash polytope and Kuhn poker -- we find that (i) selection is determined by the algorithm, not the seed, but families differ only on asymmetric Nash sets; (ii) regularized last-iterate methods (R-NaD, magnetic mirror descent) select the maximum-entropy member, the information projection of their uniform reference onto the Nash set -- exactly on the 2-D polytope and at 99.7% of maximum entropy in Kuhn -- while regret-averaging methods (CFR, CFR+, fictitious play) drift to a lower-entropy face; we confirm this on a randomized 180-game ensemble, where R-NaD attains the maximum-entropy member in 100% of converged games while CFR+ sits strictly below it in 94% (paired Wilcoxon p < 10^-27); (iii) the selected member has downstream consequences against sub-optimal opponents that scale with sequential/hidden-information structure but stay bounded -- in Kuhn the max-entropy member is a strictly better hedge, whereas on the matrix games the members differ without either dominating. We also report two negative results correcting common intuitions: removing CFR's positive-orthant (max(R,0)) projection does not eliminate boundary drift; and R-NaD's selection is anchor-following, not initialization-independent. We state the maximum-entropy / I-projection characterization as a strongly data-supported conjecture, checked throughout against analytic ground truth.

signals.title: An Interpretable, Controllable Time-Varying IIR Denoiser for On-Device Assistive Hearing
signals.summary: We present TVF (Time-Varying Filtering), an interpretable, low-latency speech enhancement model for real-time, on-device assistive hearing. A lightweight neural controller predicts, in real time, the coefficients of a differentiable cascade of 35 second-order IIR filters (biquads), so the model tracks non-stationary noise while keeping a fully interpretable processing chain: every spectral modification is an explicit, adjustable equalizer curve rather than an opaque `black-box' transform. Because the biquad cascade carries the signal processing, the controller can be made very small, driving the cascade with only 24k parameters at a 10.7ms algorithmic latency, within hearing-aid budgets, and running entirely on-device so that audio never leaves the device. We also expose the suppression-versus-preservation trade-off as an explicit control: it can be set during training through the loss weighting, and adjusted at inference, with no retraining, by mixing the noisy input with the denoised output. On hearing-aid metrics (HASPI/HASQI) the 24k model stays within about 0.02 of DFNet3 (2.3M parameters, almost two orders of magnitude larger) while using about 29X fewer multiply-accumulates, although larger black-box models still lead on reference metrics such as PESQ. We present TVF as a proof of concept for a compact, interpretable, and controllable denoiser for on-device assistive hearing.

support_evidence.title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
support_evidence.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

support_evidence.title: SRMA-Mamba: Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention Network for Pathological Liver Segmentation in MRI Volumes
support_evidence.summary: Liver cirrhosis plays a critical role in the prognosis of chronic liver disease. Early detection and timely intervention are essential for reducing mortality rates. However, the intricate anatomical architecture and diverse pathological changes of liver tissue complicate the accurate detection and characterization of pathological liver structures in clinical settings. Existing methods underutilize spatial anatomical details in volumetric MRI data, thereby hindering their clinical effectiveness and explainability. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel Mamba-based network, SRMA-Mamba, designed to model the spatial relationships within complex anatomical structures of MRI volumes. By integrating the Spatial Anatomy-Based Mamba module (SABMamba), SRMA-Mamba performs selective Mamba scans within pathological liver tissues and combines anatomical information from the sagittal, coronal, and axial planes to construct a global spatial context representation, enabling efficient volumetric segmentation of pathological liver structures. Furthermore, we introduce the Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention module (SRMA), designed to progressively refine boundary details in the segmentation map, utilizing both the coarse segmentation map and hierarchical encoding features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SRMA-Mamba surpasses state-of-the-art methods, delivering exceptional performance in 3D pathological liver segmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/JunZengz/SRMA-Mamba.

support_evidence.title: Symmetry-Aware Transformer Training for Automated Planning
support_evidence.summary: While transformers excel in many settings, their application in the field of automated planning is limited. Prior work like PlanGPT, a state-of-the-art decoder-only transformer, struggles with extrapolation from easy to hard planning problems. This in turn stems from problem symmetries: planning tasks can be represented with arbitrary variable names that carry no meaning beyond being identifiers. This causes a combinatorial explosion of equivalent representations that pure transformers cannot efficiently learn from. We propose a novel contrastive learning objective to make transformers symmetry-aware and thereby compensate for their lack of inductive bias. Combining this with architectural improvements, we show that transformers can be efficiently trained for either plan-generation or heuristic-prediction. Our results across multiple planning domains demonstrate that our symmetry-aware training effectively and efficiently addresses the limitations of PlanGPT.

support_evidence.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

support_evidence.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
support_evidence.summary:
Artificial intelligence is driving a revolution in scientific discovery, accelerating everything from hypothesis generation to mathematical theorem proving. However, this rapid acceleration is creating a systemic challenge: traditional human peer review cannot scale to match the influx of AI-assisted science. Ultimately, to resolve this tension, we must also deploy AI to accelerate the verification and review process itself. To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

support_evidence.title: Which Nash Equilibrium? Solver-Dependent Selection on Zero-Sum Nash Polytopes
support_evidence.summary: Many two-player zero-sum games admit not a unique Nash equilibrium but a convex set of them: a polytope of profiles that all share the minimax value V* yet prescribe different behaviour. Standard solvers each converge to some equilibrium and are treated as interchangeable. We ask whether they instead select different members of the Nash set, systematically as a function of the algorithm rather than the seed. Using a tabular, exactly solvable testbed of six games with analytically known Nash sets -- including a two-dimensional Nash polytope and Kuhn poker -- we find that (i) selection is determined by the algorithm, not the seed, but families differ only on asymmetric Nash sets; (ii) regularized last-iterate methods (R-NaD, magnetic mirror descent) select the maximum-entropy member, the information projection of their uniform reference onto the Nash set -- exactly on the 2-D polytope and at 99.7% of maximum entropy in Kuhn -- while regret-averaging methods (CFR, CFR+, fictitious play) drift to a lower-entropy face; we confirm this on a randomized 180-game ensemble, where R-NaD attains the maximum-entropy member in 100% of converged games while CFR+ sits strictly below it in 94% (paired Wilcoxon p < 10^-27); (iii) the selected member has downstream consequences against sub-optimal opponents that scale with sequential/hidden-information structure but stay bounded -- in Kuhn the max-entropy member is a strictly better hedge, whereas on the matrix games the members differ without either dominating. We also report two negative results correcting common intuitions: removing CFR's positive-orthant (max(R,0)) projection does not eliminate boundary drift; and R-NaD's selection is anchor-following, not initialization-independent. We state the maximum-entropy / I-projection characterization as a strongly data-supported conjecture, checked throughout against analytic ground truth.

direct_support.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
direct_support.summary: Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
support_evidence.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

related_context.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
related_context.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

related_context.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
related_context.summary: Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

related_context.title: When are LLMs Sufficient Policy Optimizers for Sequential RL Tasks?
related_context.summary: We study when large language models (LLMs) can serve as effective black-box policy optimizers for reinforcement learning (RL) tasks, i.e., when can we replace classical RL algorithms with an LLM? We explore this question by introducing Prompted Policy Optimization (PromptPO), an iterative method that prompts an LLM with Python descriptions of the state space, action space, and reward function, then has it generate and refine executable policies based on rollout feedback. Across hard exploration environments, Meta-World robotics tasks, and several real-world control problems, PromptPO often matches or exceeds the performance of standard RL baselines while using substantially fewer environment interactions. To maximize expected return, and without further explicit prompting, the policies PromptPO outputs range from tuned proportional controllers or rule-based plans to policies that run planning algorithms like value iteration. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based policy optimization is sufficient when the LLM can leverage prior knowledge about the environment or optimization strategy. PromptPO underperforms standard RL baselines in MuJoCo domains. This demonstrates possible limitations of LLM-based policy optimization to settings that requiring fine-grained continuous control.

support_evidence.title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
support_evidence.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

support_evidence.title: SRMA-Mamba: Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention Network for Pathological Liver Segmentation in MRI Volumes
support_evidence.summary: Liver cirrhosis plays a critical role in the prognosis of chronic liver disease. Early detection and timely intervention are essential for reducing mortality rates. However, the intricate anatomical architecture and diverse pathological changes of liver tissue complicate the accurate detection and characterization of pathological liver structures in clinical settings. Existing methods underutilize spatial anatomical details in volumetric MRI data, thereby hindering their clinical effectiveness and explainability. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel Mamba-based network, SRMA-Mamba, designed to model the spatial relationships within complex anatomical structures of MRI volumes. By integrating the Spatial Anatomy-Based Mamba module (SABMamba), SRMA-Mamba performs selective Mamba scans within pathological liver tissues and combines anatomical information from the sagittal, coronal, and axial planes to construct a global spatial context representation, enabling efficient volumetric segmentation of pathological liver structures. Furthermore, we introduce the Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention module (SRMA), designed to progressively refine boundary details in the segmentation map, utilizing both the coarse segmentation map and hierarchical encoding features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SRMA-Mamba surpasses state-of-the-art methods, delivering exceptional performance in 3D pathological liver segmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/JunZengz/SRMA-Mamba.

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
support_evidence.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

related_context.title: An Interpretable, Controllable Time-Varying IIR Denoiser for On-Device Assistive Hearing
related_context.summary: We present TVF (Time-Varying Filtering), an interpretable, low-latency speech enhancement model for real-time, on-device assistive hearing. A lightweight neural controller predicts, in real time, the coefficients of a differentiable cascade of 35 second-order IIR filters (biquads), so the model tracks non-stationary noise while keeping a fully interpretable processing chain: every spectral modification is an explicit, adjustable equalizer curve rather than an opaque `black-box' transform. Because the biquad cascade carries the signal processing, the controller can be made very small, driving the cascade with only 24k parameters at a 10.7ms algorithmic latency, within hearing-aid budgets, and running entirely on-device so that audio never leaves the device. We also expose the suppression-versus-preservation trade-off as an explicit control: it can be set during training through the loss weighting, and adjusted at inference, with no retraining, by mixing the noisy input with the denoised output. On hearing-aid metrics (HASPI/HASQI) the 24k model stays within about 0.02 of DFNet3 (2.3M parameters, almost two orders of magnitude larger) while using about 29X fewer multiply-accumulates, although larger black-box models still lead on reference metrics such as PESQ. We present TVF as a proof of concept for a compact, interpretable, and controllable denoiser for on-device assistive hearing.

related_context.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
related_context.summary: Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

support_evidence.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
support_evidence.summary:
Artificial intelligence is driving a revolution in scientific discovery, accelerating everything from hypothesis generation to mathematical theorem proving. However, this rapid acceleration is creating a systemic challenge: traditional human peer review cannot scale to match the influx of AI-assisted science. Ultimately, to resolve this tension, we must also deploy AI to accelerate the verification and review process itself. To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

signals.title: Memora: A Harmonic Memory Representation Balancing Abstraction and Specificity
signals.summary:
AI agents can't remember past conversations. They must constantly reload or retrieve context, which grows less efficient as tasks get longer and more complex. Memora solves this with a scalable memory system separating what’s stored from how it's retrieved.

The post Memora: A Harmonic Memory Representation Balancing Abstraction and Specificity appeared first on Microsoft Research.

support_evidence.title: Data Formulator 0.7: AI-powered data analytics for enterprise data
support_evidence.summary:
Data Formulator introduces AI-powered analytics for enterprise data workflows. Data teams can easily bring enterprise data into an AI-ready workspace where users can explore, analyze, and visualize data with AI agents to turn raw data into actionable insights.

The post Data Formulator 0.7: AI-powered data analytics for enterprise data appeared first on Microsoft Research.

support_evidence.title: Extending Human Intelligence Through AI
support_evidence.summary:
Understanding AI as an extension of human intelligence—not a replacement for it—offers a more grounded path for building trustworthy AI systems.

support_evidence.summary:

support_evidence.title: Talos: Scaling rare disease diagnosis with automated, iterative genomic reanalysis
support_evidence.summary:
Talos was built to help resolve a major bottleneck in genomic medicine: human review time. The open-source system recovered 90% of in-scope diagnoses while surfacing just 1.3 candidate variants per patient for expert review.

The post Talos: Scaling rare disease diagnosis with automated, iterative genomic reanalysis appeared first on Microsoft Research.

signals.title: ERNIE-5.1-Preview Tops LMArena Text Leaderboard as No.1 Chinese Model!
signals.summary: On April 30, LMArena released its latest rankings. ERNIE-5.1-Preview ranked No. 1 among Chinese models and No. 13 globally on the LMArena Text Arena, placing in the global top 10 across multiple category leaderboards.

support_evidence.title: ERNIE-5.0 Tops LMArena Text Leaderboard as No.1 Chinese Model!
support_evidence.summary: On January 15, LMArena released its latest rankings. ERNIE-5.0-0110 achieved a score of 1,460, ranking No. 1 among Chinese models and No. 8 globally on the LMArena Text Arena.

support_evidence.title: ERNIE 5.0: A 2.4 Trillion-Parameter Unified Multimodal Foundation Model
support_evidence.summary: We introduce ERNIE 5.0: a 2.4 trillion-parameter Unified Multimodal Model trained from scratch. Integrating text, image, video, and audio into a single autoregressive framework, it overcomes the limitations of late-fusion architectures to achieve seamless cross-modal understanding and generation.

support_evidence.title: Introducing ERNIE-Image
support_evidence.summary: ERNIE-Image is a text-to-image generation model built on a single-stream Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with 8B DiT parameters, achieving leading performance among open-weights models.

support_evidence.title: ERNIE 5.1 Officially Released! Topping Multiple Leaderboards — A Model That Writes Better and Understands You More
support_evidence.summary: ERNIE 5.1 is officially released, achieving leading performance at only 6% of the pre-training cost of comparable models. Powered by disaggregated fully-asynchronous reinforcement learning and scaled agentic post-training, ERNIE 5.1 delivers comprehensive upgrades across Agent, reasoning, and creative capabilities, ranking 1st in China on the Arena Search Arena.

support_evidence.title: PaddleOCR-VL-1.5: Towards a Multi-Task 0.9B VLM for Robust In-the-Wild Document Parsing
support_evidence.summary: 🚀 We release PaddleOCR-VL-1.5, an upgraded model achieving a new state-of-the-art (SOTA)accuracy of 94.5% on OmniDocBench v1.5.

direct_support.summary: On January 15, LMArena released its latest rankings. ERNIE-5.0-0110 achieved a score of 1,460, ranking No. 1 among Chinese models and No. 8 globally on the LMArena Text Arena.

signals.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
signals.summary: Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

support_evidence.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
support_evidence.summary:
Artificial intelligence is driving a revolution in scientific discovery, accelerating everything from hypothesis generation to mathematical theorem proving. However, this rapid acceleration is creating a systemic challenge: traditional human peer review cannot scale to match the influx of AI-assisted science. Ultimately, to resolve this tension, we must also deploy AI to accelerate the verification and review process itself. To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
support_evidence.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

support_evidence.title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
support_evidence.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

support_evidence.title: When are LLMs Sufficient Policy Optimizers for Sequential RL Tasks?
support_evidence.summary: We study when large language models (LLMs) can serve as effective black-box policy optimizers for reinforcement learning (RL) tasks, i.e., when can we replace classical RL algorithms with an LLM? We explore this question by introducing Prompted Policy Optimization (PromptPO), an iterative method that prompts an LLM with Python descriptions of the state space, action space, and reward function, then has it generate and refine executable policies based on rollout feedback. Across hard exploration environments, Meta-World robotics tasks, and several real-world control problems, PromptPO often matches or exceeds the performance of standard RL baselines while using substantially fewer environment interactions. To maximize expected return, and without further explicit prompting, the policies PromptPO outputs range from tuned proportional controllers or rule-based plans to policies that run planning algorithms like value iteration. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based policy optimization is sufficient when the LLM can leverage prior knowledge about the environment or optimization strategy. PromptPO underperforms standard RL baselines in MuJoCo domains. This demonstrates possible limitations of LLM-based policy optimization to settings that requiring fine-grained continuous control.

signals.title: SRMA-Mamba: Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention Network for Pathological Liver Segmentation in MRI Volumes
signals.summary: Liver cirrhosis plays a critical role in the prognosis of chronic liver disease. Early detection and timely intervention are essential for reducing mortality rates. However, the intricate anatomical architecture and diverse pathological changes of liver tissue complicate the accurate detection and characterization of pathological liver structures in clinical settings. Existing methods underutilize spatial anatomical details in volumetric MRI data, thereby hindering their clinical effectiveness and explainability. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel Mamba-based network, SRMA-Mamba, designed to model the spatial relationships within complex anatomical structures of MRI volumes. By integrating the Spatial Anatomy-Based Mamba module (SABMamba), SRMA-Mamba performs selective Mamba scans within pathological liver tissues and combines anatomical information from the sagittal, coronal, and axial planes to construct a global spatial context representation, enabling efficient volumetric segmentation of pathological liver structures. Furthermore, we introduce the Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention module (SRMA), designed to progressively refine boundary details in the segmentation map, utilizing both the coarse segmentation map and hierarchical encoding features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SRMA-Mamba surpasses state-of-the-art methods, delivering exceptional performance in 3D pathological liver segmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/JunZengz/SRMA-Mamba.

support_evidence.title: Symmetry-Aware Transformer Training for Automated Planning
support_evidence.summary: While transformers excel in many settings, their application in the field of automated planning is limited. Prior work like PlanGPT, a state-of-the-art decoder-only transformer, struggles with extrapolation from easy to hard planning problems. This in turn stems from problem symmetries: planning tasks can be represented with arbitrary variable names that carry no meaning beyond being identifiers. This causes a combinatorial explosion of equivalent representations that pure transformers cannot efficiently learn from. We propose a novel contrastive learning objective to make transformers symmetry-aware and thereby compensate for their lack of inductive bias. Combining this with architectural improvements, we show that transformers can be efficiently trained for either plan-generation or heuristic-prediction. Our results across multiple planning domains demonstrate that our symmetry-aware training effectively and efficiently addresses the limitations of PlanGPT.

support_evidence.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution

support_evidence.title: An Interpretable, Controllable Time-Varying IIR Denoiser for On-Device Assistive Hearing
support_evidence.summary: We present TVF (Time-Varying Filtering), an interpretable, low-latency speech enhancement model for real-time, on-device assistive hearing. A lightweight neural controller predicts, in real time, the coefficients of a differentiable cascade of 35 second-order IIR filters (biquads), so the model tracks non-stationary noise while keeping a fully interpretable processing chain: every spectral modification is an explicit, adjustable equalizer curve rather than an opaque `black-box' transform. Because the biquad cascade carries the signal processing, the controller can be made very small, driving the cascade with only 24k parameters at a 10.7ms algorithmic latency, within hearing-aid budgets, and running entirely on-device so that audio never leaves the device. We also expose the suppression-versus-preservation trade-off as an explicit control: it can be set during training through the loss weighting, and adjusted at inference, with no retraining, by mixing the noisy input with the denoised output. On hearing-aid metrics (HASPI/HASQI) the 24k model stays within about 0.02 of DFNet3 (2.3M parameters, almost two orders of magnitude larger) while using about 29X fewer multiply-accumulates, although larger black-box models still lead on reference metrics such as PESQ. We present TVF as a proof of concept for a compact, interpretable, and controllable denoiser for on-device assistive hearing.

PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. direct_support.title: Symmetry-Aware Transformer Training for Automated Planning
direct_support.summary: While transformers excel in many settings, their application in the field of automated planning is limited. Prior work like PlanGPT, a state-of-the-art decoder-only transformer, struggles with extrapolation from easy to hard planning problems. This in turn stems from problem symmetries: planning tasks can be represented with arbitrary variable names that carry no meaning beyond being identifiers. This causes a combinatorial explosion of equivalent representations that pure transformers cannot efficiently learn from. We propose a novel contrastive learning objective to make transformers symmetry-aware and thereby compensate for their lack of inductive bias. Combining this with architectural improvements, we show that transformers can be efficiently trained for either plan-generation or heuristic-prediction. Our results across multiple planning domains demonstrate that our symmetry-aware training effectively and efficiently addresses the limitations of PlanGPT.

direct_support.title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
direct_support.summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

direct_support.title: An Interpretable, Controllable Time-Varying IIR Denoiser for On-Device Assistive Hearing
direct_support.summary: We present TVF (Time-Varying Filtering), an interpretable, low-latency speech enhancement model for real-time, on-device assistive hearing. Because the biquad cascade carries the signal processing, the controller can be made very small, driving the cascade with only 24k parameters at a 10.7ms algorithmic latency, within hearing-aid budgets, and running entirely on-device so that audio never leaves the device. We also expose the suppression-versus-preservation trade-off as an explicit control: it can be set during training through the loss weighting, and adjusted at inference, with no retraining, by mixing the noisy input with the denoised output. On hearing-aid metrics (HASPI/HASQI) the 24k model stays within about 0.02 of DFNet3 (2.3M parameters, almost two orders of magnitude larger) while using about 29X fewer multiply-accumulates, although larger black-box models still lead on reference metrics such as PESQ. We present TVF as a proof of concept for a compact, interpretable, and controllable denoiser for on-device assistive hearing.

direct_support.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
direct_support.summary: Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

signals.title: Which Nash Equilibrium? Solver-Dependent Selection on Zero-Sum Nash Polytopes
signals.summary: Many two-player zero-sum games admit not a unique Nash equilibrium but a convex set of them: a polytope of profiles that all share the minimax value V* yet prescribe different behaviour. Standard solvers each converge to some equilibrium and are treated as interchangeable. We ask whether they instead select different members of the Nash set, systematically as a function of the algorithm rather than the seed. Using a tabular, exactly solvable testbed of six games with analytically known Nash sets -- including a two-dimensional Nash polytope and Kuhn poker -- we find that (i) selection is determined by the algorithm, not the seed, but families differ only on asymmetric Nash sets; (ii) regularized last-iterate methods (R-NaD, magnetic mirror descent) select the maximum-entropy member, the information projection of their uniform reference onto the Nash set -- exactly on the 2-D polytope and at 99.7% of maximum entropy in Kuhn -- while regret-averaging methods (CFR, CFR+, fictitious play) drift to a lower-entropy face; we confirm this on a randomized 180-game ensemble, where R-NaD attains the maximum-entropy member in 100% of converged games while CFR+ sits strictly below it in 94% (paired Wilcoxon p < 10^-27); (iii) the selected member has downstream consequences against sub-optimal opponents that scale with sequential/hidden-information structure but stay bounded -- in Kuhn the max-entropy member is a strictly better hedge, whereas on the matrix games the members differ without either dominating. We also report two negative results correcting common intuitions: removing CFR's positive-orthant (max(R,0)) projection does not eliminate boundary drift; and R-NaD's selection is anchor-following, not initialization-independent. We state the maximum-entropy / I-projection characterization as a strongly data-supported conjecture, checked throughout against analytic ground truth.

support_evidence.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
support_evidence.summary:
Artificial intelligence is driving a revolution in scientific discovery, accelerating everything from hypothesis generation to mathematical theorem proving. However, this rapid acceleration is creating a systemic challenge: traditional human peer review cannot scale to match the influx of AI-assisted science. Ultimately, to resolve this tension, we must also deploy AI to accelerate the verification and review process itself. To frame the discussion around this transition, we propose a taxonomy consisting of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation, and discuss various trade-offs involved with each.
  As a step toward this future, we introduce the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework built for deep scientific review and verification. PAT ingests full scientific manuscripts and produces a comprehensive evaluation, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying potential flaws. By utilizing inference scaling techniques, PAT is able to identify deeper issues than a single model call alone, achieving a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on mathematical errors in the SPOT benchmark. Pilot deployments of PAT as a pre-submission tool for authors at two major Computer Science conferences -- STOC and ICML -- demonstrate its ability to identify critical errors and suggest substantive improvements to research papers. By catching errors early, PAT eases the cognitive burden placed on referees, while preserving their control over the outcomes of the review process.

support_evidence.title: SRMA-Mamba: Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention Network for Pathological Liver Segmentation in MRI Volumes
support_evidence.summary: Liver cirrhosis plays a critical role in the prognosis of chronic liver disease. Early detection and timely intervention are essential for reducing mortality rates. However, the intricate anatomical architecture and diverse pathological changes of liver tissue complicate the accurate detection and characterization of pathological liver structures in clinical settings. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel Mamba-based network, SRMA-Mamba, designed to model the spatial relationships within complex anatomical structures of MRI volumes. By integrating the Spatial Anatomy-Based Mamba module (SABMamba), SRMA-Mamba performs selective Mamba scans within pathological liver tissues and combines anatomical information from the sagittal, coronal, and axial planes to construct a global spatial context representation, enabling efficient volumetric segmentation of pathological liver structures. Furthermore, we introduce the Spatial Reverse Mamba Attention module (SRMA), designed to progressively refine boundary details in the segmentation map, utilizing both the coarse segmentation map and hierarchical encoding features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SRMA-Mamba surpasses state-of-the-art methods, delivering exceptional performance in 3D pathological liver segmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/JunZengz/SRMA-Mamba.

support_evidence.title: When are LLMs Sufficient Policy Optimizers for Sequential RL Tasks?
support_evidence.summary: We study when large language models (LLMs) can serve as effective black-box policy optimizers for reinforcement learning (RL) tasks, i.e., when can we replace classical RL algorithms with an LLM? We explore this question by introducing Prompted Policy Optimization (PromptPO), an iterative method that prompts an LLM with Python descriptions of the state space, action space, and reward function, then has it generate and refine executable policies based on rollout feedback. Across hard exploration environments, Meta-World robotics tasks, and several real-world control problems, PromptPO often matches or exceeds the performance of standard RL baselines while using substantially fewer environment interactions. To maximize expected return, and without further explicit prompting, the policies PromptPO outputs range from tuned proportional controllers or rule-based plans to policies that run planning algorithms like value iteration. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based policy optimization is sufficient when the LLM can leverage prior knowledge about the environment or optimization strategy. PromptPO underperforms standard RL baselines in MuJoCo domains. This demonstrates possible limitations of LLM-based policy optimization to settings that requiring fine-grained continuous control.

support_evidence.title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
support_evidence.summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.
